exercises.title: Stack Simulation
exercises.description: Simulate a simple browser history stack. Append three URLs, then pop the last two and print them. Finally print the remaining history.
exercises.starterCode:
history = []

# TODO: Append these three URLs to history:
# "https://python.org"
# "https://docs.python.org"
# "https://pypi.org"

# TODO: Pop and print the last visited URL

# TODO: Pop and print the second-to-last visited URL

# TODO: Print the remaining history
hints: Use history.append(url) to add each URL
history.pop() removes and returns the last item
Store the popped value in a variable and print it
print(history) at the end shows what remains

exercises.title: Sort a Leaderboard
exercises.description: Given a list of (name, score) tuples, sort by score descending, then print each entry as 'rank. name: score'.
exercises.starterCode:
scores = [
    ("Alice", 9500),
    ("Bob", 8700),
    ("Carol", 9800),
    ("David", 8700),
]

# TODO: Sort scores by score value descending
# Hint: use sorted() with a lambda key

# TODO: Print each entry with rank, name, and score
# Output format: "1. Carol: 9800"
hints: sorted(scores, key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True) sorts by the second element descending
Use enumerate(sorted_scores, 1) to get 1-based rank and item
Unpack each item as (name, score) inside the for loop

exercises.title: List Search and Count
exercises.description: Given a list of numbers with duplicates, find: the index of the first occurrence of 20, the count of how many times 20 appears, and all unique values (preserving order) using a seen set.
exercises.starterCode:
numbers = [10, 20, 30, 20, 40, 20, 10, 50]

# TODO: Print the index of the first occurrence of 20

# TODO: Print how many times 20 appears in the list

# TODO: Build and print a list of unique values, preserving order
# Hint: iterate with a 'seen' set and check before appending
hints: Use seen = set() and append to unique only if item not in seen

warmup.title: Quick Recall: append vs extend
warmup.description: What does this code print? Pay attention to the difference between append and extend.
warmup.starterCode:
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [4, 5]
a.append(b)
print(a)

c = [1, 2, 3]
c.extend(b)
print(c)
